ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Pindi Chole; mapped to the closest parent class 'chickpea dish' (FOODON_03311737). Pindi Chole is a dry-spiced Punjabi chickpea preparation distinct from generic chana masala.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chickpeas, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. Pindi Chole includes additional spices, oil tempering, and natural coloring agents (tea, amla) not captured in the base code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Pindi Chole or a sufficiently close chickpea dish preparation.

preparation_method: natural coloring with tea leaves and amla

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Pindi Chole is approximately 350 g (1 plate), typically accompanied by bhature, puri, or whole wheat roti.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from NIN ICMR data for cooked chickpeas (Bengal gram / kabuli chana) with adjustment for added oil (~5 g per 100 g cooked dish), spices, and black salt. The NIN database is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chickpeas, cooked, boiled, without salt; FDC ID 173757) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — chickpeas / garbanzo beans, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 32 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (kabuli chana). Pindi Chole is a dry-spiced preparation of the same chickpeas; the high dietary fibre content, addition of amla (vitamin C), and ajwain are consistent with maintaining a low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (32 × 17.1 g per 100 g × 3.5 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 19.2 for a 350 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from black salt (kala namak) and added salt; partially offset by high potassium (290 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g and request reduced salt and black salt during preparation, as the sodium content of 214 mg/100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets; the high potassium content provides a partial counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 32) and high dietary fibre (4.3 g/100 g) supporting slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pindi Chole is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of 32 and high fibre content; it should be paired with whole grain breads (roti, whole wheat puri) rather than refined flour bhature to maintain overall meal glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and potassium-rich chickpea base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile of Pindi Chole, combined with its soluble fibre content from chickpeas, supports LDL cholesterol management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate added oil during tempering and control total portion size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, amla, ajwain) and high fibre content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory properties of ginger, amla, and ajwain in Pindi Chole may provide modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; no specific restrictions apply.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pindi Chole is a celebrated rustic Punjabi dish originating from the city of Rawalpindi in undivided Punjab (present-day Pakistan). It is distinguished from standard Chana Masala by its characteristic deep, almost black color and its dry, robust texture — it contains no liquid onion-tomato gravy. The dark hue is achieved by boiling dried chickpeas (kabuli chana) together with tea leaves and dried amla (Indian gooseberry), which also impart a subtle tangy depth. The flavor profile is complex: smoky, earthy, and tangy, built from a special blend of dry-roasted whole spices including anardana (dried pomegranate seeds), black salt (kala namak), cumin, coriander, and a heavy tempering of carom seeds (ajwain) and fresh ginger juliennes in hot oil. The dish is cooked using a bhuna technique — slow sautéing until all moisture evaporates and the spices coat the chickpeas thoroughly. Nutritionally, Pindi Chole is a high-fibre, moderate-protein, plant-based main dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 137 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 5.1 g fat, 17.1 g carbohydrate, and 4.3 g dietary fibre. It has a very low glycemic index of 32, making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (214 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertension patients. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Pindi Chole is traditionally served with bhature (deep-fried leavened bread), puri, or whole wheat roti, and is a staple of Punjabi festive and everyday dining.
common_user_queries: How is Pindi Chole different from Chana Masala?
Is Pindi Chole good for diabetics?
Is Pindi Chole healthy for hypertension?
What spices are in Pindi Chole?
Why is Pindi Chole black in color?
meal_context: street food staple in North India and Pakistan

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from NIN ICMR data for cooked chickpeas with oil adjustment, as no exact Pindi Chole entry exists in any approved primary source. The GI value is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas, which is the direct base ingredient. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact spice blend, oil quantity, and preparation variation introduce uncertainty in the final nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN ICMR (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) for cooked kabuli chana with approximately 5 g added oil per 100 g of finished dish, consistent with the bhuna preparation style. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173757, chickpeas cooked boiled without salt) for plausibility of cholesterol and saturated fat values. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this spiced chickpea preparation is not available in NIN ICMR or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the finished dry-style Pindi Chole preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a plant-oil-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for chickpeas cooked with vegetable oil. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero for a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero/negligible for this plant-based preparation; confirmed by NIN and USDA sources. 4. Portions: the input contained duplicate '1 serving (200g)' entries; deduplicated to one default portion of 350 g (1 plate) and one alternate of 200 g. 5. GI source: the 'Journal of Food Science and Technology' cited in the input is not on the approved GI source list; replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI evidence source. 6. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Pindi Chole in any of the three ontology systems reviewed.
